Words/Phrases Learned: สวัสดี (sà-wàt-dii) = hello; ขอบคุณ (khàawp-khun) = thank you; ครับ (khráp) = male polite ending; ค่ะ (khâ) = female statement ending; คะ (khá) = female question ending; ขอโทษ (khǎaw-thôot) = excuse me / sorry; ไม่เข้าใจ (mâi khâo-jai) = I do not understand; พูดช้าๆ ได้ไหม (phûut cháa-cháa dâai mái) = can you speak slowly?
Grammar Learned: Teach pragmatic greeting and gratitude with politeness particles.; Request slower speech without breaking politeness.; Primary objective: Learner can handle first-contact interactions politely in Thai.; Secondary objective: Learner can produce clear survival lines with correct politeness particles and usable tone-marked transliteration.; Polite endings: ครับ/ค่ะ | khráp/khâ | polite sentence endings; Transliteration must follow PTM-adapted scheme with inline tone marks (no superscript tone letters).; Dropping politeness particles in formal first-contact situations.; Saying khop-khun without low tone marker (must be khàawp-khun in this system).; Thai sentence pragmatics in real interactions (meaning from context + particles).

Words/Phrases Learned: ไก่ (gài) = chicken; ไข่ (khài) = egg; ใกล้ (glâi) = near; ไกล (glǎi) = far; ขา (khǎa) = leg; ค่า (khâa) = value / fee
Grammar Learned: Lock in short/long vowel distinction.; Primary objective: Distinguish consonants, short/long vowels, and 5 tones at a beginner level.; Scenario: Learner listens to Thai words that sound similar but differ by tone/vowel length.; Thai sound inventory basics (consonants, vowels, tone-bearing syllables).

Words/Phrases Learned: สวัสดี (sà-wàt-dii) = hello; ขอบคุณ (khàawp-khun) = thank you; ขอโทษ (khǎaw-thôot) = excuse me / sorry; ไม่เข้าใจ (mâi khâo-jai) = I do not understand; พูดช้าๆ ได้ไหม (phûut cháa-cháa dâai mái) = can you speak slowly?
Grammar Learned: Baseline politeness entry; Study method meta-grammar: production-first drills, review cadence, and role-play structure.
Mid-class consonants and their role in tone rule calculation.
High vs low consonant classes and resulting tone behavior.
Short vs long vowel contrasts and pronunciation meaning changes.
